html: IFC = International Finance Corporation (World Bank Group). ADB = Asian Development Bank. NBD = Emirates NBD (UAE-based bank, full name Emirates NBD Bank PJSC). Foreign involvement: Emirates NBD (Dubai) is acquiring ~60%+ controlling stake (~$3B primary + open offer) with RBI nod April 2026—largest FDI in Indian banking. Legacy DFIs (IFC, ADB, British International Investment) hold low single-digit %. FPIs ~20-28% (global funds, no single dominant name). Board & Indian side: Professional—Chairman Chandan Sinha (ex-RBI), MD/CEO R Subramaniakumar. No promoter holding; top owners are domestic mutual funds/DIIs (~35-40%). No prominent political or specific corporate family ties in latest filings or board per public records. Details on rbl.bank/investors.

text:
RBL Bank has 0% promoter holding. It's institutionally owned—top shareholders are domestic mutual funds/DIIs (~35-40%), FPIs (~20-28%), and legacy DFIs like British International Investment, IFC, ADB (low single digits).

Big recent corporate tie: Emirates NBD (UAE bank) is acquiring controlling stake (~60% via $3B primary infusion + open offer), with RBI nod already in. 

No prominent Indian political figures or direct political connections on board or in major ownership per public records. Board is led by pros like Chairman Chandan Sinha (ex-RBI). 

Details in their latest shareholding filings on rbl.bank.

post:
The Claude agent deleted the entire production database and all backups.

One prompt. One bad permission. Total annihilation.

AI agents are extremely powerful but without guardrails in production = nuclear risk.

Solution:
• Put all agents in a sandbox
• Default read-only
• Human approval for destructive actions
• Multi-layer permission system
post_highlights: Solution: • Put all agents in a sandbox • Default read-only • Human approval for destructive actions • Multi-layer permission system • AI agents are extremely powerful but without guardrails in production = nuclear risk. • The Claude agent deleted the entire production database and all backups.

text:
Emirates NBD reported a 3% year-on-year rise in its quarterly profit, marking its first financial update since the recent Middle East conflict. Profit reached 6.4 billion dirhams (USD 1.74 billion), supported by steady deposit growth and a stronger balance sheet. The bank recorded increases in loans, deposits, and total assets, which crossed 1.22 trillion dirhams. However, the lender also took a precautionary impairment charge of 865 million dirhams, reflecting cautious provisioning. Analysts noted potential pressure on growth due to regional uncertainties, although the bank’s financial position remains stable.

But in Dubai, your money follows a controlled path

It goes into a regulated escrow account
And only gets released when construction actually moves forward https://t.co/Fma7CgPAAx
html:
Off plan just got more predictable

You can now secure mortgage pre approval from day one not handover

Thanks to Emirates NBD and Dubai Holding

Better planning, less uncertainty
But approvals are more selective and not for all projects

text:
Emirates NBD issuing a $1bn AT1 bond isn't about bad loans.

It's about REPLENISHING DOLLARS.

Capital flight + no oil exports + no tourism = dollars leaving faster than they arrive. 

Either the UAE central bank can provide banks with dollars from its reserves (and there are plenty of reserve) or it can ask the banks to float bonds. This may be just testing the water for the latter option.

text:
Emirates NBD advances dollar bond sale amid war on Iran
——
Emirates NBD, Dubai’s largest bank by assets, said on Monday it is moving forward with a US dollar-denominated bond sale, becoming the first Gulf issuer to signal plans to access public debt markets since the start of the US-Israeli war on Iran.

The bank has appointed Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank, Barclays, Citi, Emirates NBD Capital, First Abu Dhabi Bank, HSBC and JPMorgan to organize investor meetings on Monday ahead of a potential issuance of a US dollar-denominated non-call six-year Additional Tier 1 (AT1) bond.

It added that it will hold in-person investor meetings in London alongside virtual calls for the planned benchmark bond, which is typically valued at a minimum of $500 million.

text:
It seems to me that the UAE central bank has likely done stress tests on the major banks and found certain capital vulnerabilities (probably tied to real estate exposure), and quietly encouraged ENBD to be the first to market to "test the waters."

If the ENBD deal prices well, other banks will follow. If it struggles, the central bank will need to find another way to recapitalize the system—potentially drawing on sovereign wealth funds.
